perturbation.original_text: Party A shall also cooperate with Party B to market Baidu promotion services through the use of search engine. In other words, it is a technical service in which Party B displays the website of Party B's clients on the relevant page of Baidu website and/or on the pages and / or interfaces of any other Baidu union members.
perturbation.changed_text: Party A should consider cooperating with Party B to market Baidu promotion services through the use of search engine. In other words, it is a technical service in which Party B may display the website of Party B's clients on the relevant page of Baidu website and/or on the pages and / or interfaces of any other Baidu union members.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'Party A shall' to 'Party A should consider' weakens the obligation, making the cooperation discretionary rather than mandatory. Additionally, changing 'Party B displays' to 'Party B may display' removes the obligation for Party B to actually display websites of clients, thus making the technical service conditional and ambiguous.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_citation: Article 8 of the Contract Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 8 of the Contract Law of the People's Republic of China states that legally binding contracts are protected by law. By changing 'shall' to 'should consider,' the modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the legal obligation for Party A to cooperate, potentially making the relevant section of the contract unenforceable or open to interpretation, which goes against the spirit of legally binding contracts.

Contract Law of the People's Republic of China was a law in the PRC that came into force on October 1, 1999, and was abolished on Jan 1, 2021 with the enforcement of Civil Code of the People's Republic of China . It is the main source of contract law in the PRC. The most recent version combines many parts of Chinese contract law that were previously parts of other substantive laws, and has led to increased legal uniformity. The law includes both general and specific provisions and also has the legal rules that relate to the formation, fulfilment and breach of contracts. The law allows for various different types of contracts that can be entered into. [ 1 ] "The principles of the freedom of contract, of good faith, and of the fostering of transactions have informed and guided the formulation of the Contract Law and are embodied in many of its major provisions." [ 2 ] The main purpose of the law is stated in Article 1: "This Law is enacted for the purpose of protecting the legitimate rights and interests of the parties to contracts, maintaining the socio-economic order and promoting the socialist modernization." [ 3 ] Article 2 has the definition for a contract: "For the purpose of this Law, a contract means an agreement on the establis
perturbation.justification: The original contract uses the terms 'shall' and 'displays,' imposing mandatory obligations on Party A and Party B, respectively. The modified contract weakens these obligations by using 'should consider' and 'may display,' making the cooperation and display of websites discretionary. The Law Explanation states that the change from 'shall' to 'should consider' introduces ambiguity and weakens the legal obligation, making the contract unenforceable. The Perturbation Explanation also confirms the change weakens the legal obligation. The definition of the perturbation type 'Ambiguous Legal Obligation' states that a contradiction happens when an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law.

perturbation.original_text: Party A undertakes to comply with the Business Cooperation Standard of Baidu Union published in http://union.baidu.com/regAgreement.html (see Annex 3), or otherwise Party A will be deemed to breach this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Party A will generally adhere to the Business Cooperation Standard of Baidu Union published in http://union.baidu.com/regAgreement.html (see Annex 3), but deviations may occur.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'undertakes to comply' to 'will generally adhere,' the obligation is weakened. The added phrase 'but deviations may occur' introduces significant ambiguity, as it is unclear under what circumstances deviations are acceptable, making the clause difficult to enforce and potentially non-compliant with regulations requiring strict adherence to standards.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_citation: Article 60 of the Contract Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 60 of the Contract Law of the People's Republic of China requires parties to perform their obligations in good faith and in accordance with the terms of the agreement. The phrase 'but deviations may occur' allows for potential non-compliance with the Business Cooperation Standard, undermining the principle of good faith performance and creating uncertainty about the enforceable obligations.

Republic of China . It is the main source of contract law in the PRC. The most recent version combines many parts of Chinese contract law that were previously parts of other substantive laws, and has led to increased legal uniformity. The law includes both general and specific provisions and also has the legal rules that relate to the formation, fulfilment and breach of contracts. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligation by changing 'undertakes to comply' to 'will generally adhere' and adding 'but deviations may occur.' This introduces ambiguity regarding acceptable deviations from the Business Cooperation Standard. The Law Explanation states that Article 60 of the Contract Law of the People's Republic of China requires parties to perform their obligations in good faith. The changed text undermines this principle of good faith by allowing for deviations, creating uncertainty about enforceable obligations. The changed text therefore introduces an Ambiguous Legal Obligation, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law, thus creating a legal contradiction, as defined in the instructions.

perturbation.original_text: Party B warrants that it legally owns or otherwise holds a valid license to the intellectual property relating to the services or products provided pursuant to this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Party B generally believes that it legally owns or holds a license to the intellectual property relating to the services or products provided pursuant to this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'Party B warrants' to 'Party B generally believes' removes the guarantee and introduces uncertainty about Party B's ownership or licensing of intellectual property. The warranty becomes a statement of belief rather than a legally binding guarantee, which introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_citation: Article 150 of the Contract Law of the People's Republic of China
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 150 discusses implied warranties. By changing the language to a statement of belief, the warranty is significantly weakened and the burden of proof shifted, reducing the warranty to a statement of opinion. This could impede dispute resolution, and it contradicts the principle of good faith and the need for definite and clear contractual obligations under Chinese contract law.

Home User Management About Us Chinese lawinfochina.com > Laws & Regulations > Text Bookmark Download Print Search: "Fabao" Window Font Size: Rules for the Establishment of Foreign-shared Securities Companies [Expired] 外资参股证券公司设立规则 [失效] 【法宝引证码】 Issuing authority： Instrumentalities of the State Council , All Commissions , China Securities Regulatory Commission Document Number： Order No.8 of the China Securities Regulatory Commission Date issued： 06-01-2002 Effective date: 07-01-2002 Level of Authority: Departmental Rules Area of Law: Securities , Securities Revised by： Decision of the China Securities Regulatory Commission on Amending the Rules for the Establishment of Foreign-shared Securities Companies (2007) (Issued on 12-28-2007 Effective on 01-01-2008); Invalidated by： Measures for the Administration of Foreign-Funded Securities Companies (Issued on 04-28-2018 Effective on 04-28-2018); Order of the China Securities Regulatory Commission (No. 8) The Rules for the Establishment of Foreign-shared Securities Companies are hereby promulgated, and shall come into force on July 1, 2002. Chairman Zhou Xiaochuan June 1, 2002 Rules for the Establishment of Foreign-shared Securities Companies (June 1, 2002 Order No.8 of the China Securities Regulatory Commission) 中国证券监督管理委员会令 （第8号） 现发布《外资参股证券公司设立规则》，自2002年7月1日起施行。 主席 周小川 二00二年六月一日 外资参股证券公司设立规则 （2002年6月1日 中国证券监督管理委员会令第8号公布） Article 1 In order to meet the demands of opening the securities market, to strengthen and improve the supervision and administration of foreign-shared securities companies and to define the conditions and procedures for the establishment of foreign-shared securities companies, these Rules have been formulated in accordance with the relevant provisions of the Company Law and the Securities Law. 第一条 为了适应证券市场对外开放的需要，加强和完善对外资参股证券公司的监督管理，明确外资参股证券公司的设立条件和程序，根据《 公司法 》和《 证券法 》的有关规定，制定本规则。 Article 2 The foreign-shared securities companies as used in these Rules shall refer to those of which the shares are transferred to or purchased by foreign shareholders or those that are co-founded by the foreign and domestic shareholders. 第二条 本规则所称外资参股证券公司包括境外股东受让、认购境内证券公司股权而变更的证券公司或者境外股东与境内股东共同出资设立的证券公司。 Article 3 The China Securities Regulatory Commission (hereinafter referred to as the CSRC) shall be responsible for the examination and approval, supervision and administration of foreign-shared securities companies. 第三条 中国证券监督管理委员会（以下简称“中国证监会”）负责对外资参股证券公司的审批和监督管理。 Article 4 The organizational structure of a foreign-shared securities company shall be limited company. The name, registered capital, establishment and duties of the departments of a foreign-shared securities company shall be compatible with the relevant provisions of the Company Law , the Securities Law and the CSRC. 第四条 外资参股证券公司的组织形式为有限责任公司。 外资参股证券公司的名称、注册资本、组织机构的设立及职责，应当符合《 公司法 》、《 证券法 》和中国证监会的有关规定。 Article 5 A foreign-shared securities company may operate the following businesses: 第五条 外资参股证券公司可以经营下列业务： 1) Underwriting of shares (including RMB average shares and foreign shares) and bonds (including bonds issued by the government and companies); （一）股票（包括人民币普通股、外资股）和债券（包括政府债券、公司债券）的承销； 2) Brokerage of foreign shares; （二）外资股的经纪； 3) Brokerage and proprietary dealing of bonds (including bonds issued by the government and companies); （三）债券（包括政府债券、公司债券）的经纪和自营； 4) Other businesses approved by the CSRC. The foreign stocks as used in the preceding paragraph shall refer to domestic listed foreign shares (B-shares) and overseas listed foreign shares. The securities company shall file the application for business scope to the CSRC according to the first paragraph of this Article. Those engaging in the lead underwriting of stocks shall obtain the license for share lead underwriting according to the provisions of the CSRC on the administration of share lead underwriti
perturbation.justification: The original text provides a warranty from Party B regarding intellectual property ownership/licensing, while the changed text weakens this to a 'general belief.' This introduces ambiguity regarding Party B's actual legal standing, as 'generally believes' doesn't provide the same legal assurance or guarantee as a warranty. The LLM explanation indicates this shift impedes dispute resolution and potentially goes against good faith principles because the change reduces a warranty to a statement of opinion. The scraped law snippet does not explicitly address warranties or guarantees, but its emphasis on the conditions and regulations that companies need to follow implies a degree of legal responsibility that the revised statement might undermine. Therefore, the shift introduces ambiguity about the fulfillment of legal obligations and protections.
